segments.s731-g9: چنین فعل + مفعول word order: گرفتند (third-person plural past/simple perfect-form) is the main verb 'they took/uttered' used here to mean 'they pronounced/uttered' (classical usage). نفرین is the direct object 'a curse'; به + اسمِ خاص (بهرام) marks the target 'against/at Bahram'; the final بر reinforces the prepositional relation 'upon/against'. Together the clause means 'they uttered a curse against Bahram.'
segments.s731-g10: بران جام is a poetic/archaic phrase: بران here = 'that' / 'upon that' qualifying جام 'cup'. و connects the two coordinated targets. آرنده جام 'the bringer of the cup' is a noun phrase (آرنده 'bringer' + جام 'cup'). The final بر repeats the preposition 'against' (parallel to the previous clause), so the whole segment gives the second object of the curse: 'against that cup and against the cup-bearer.'
segments.s731-g11: چنین گفت که is a formula introducing reported speech ('thus he said that'). اکنون sets the time 'now'. بر + بومِ ری is a locative phrase 'on the soil/ground of Rey' (classical poetic locution). The clause so far = 'thus he said that now on the soil of Rey…'.
segments.s731-g12: به introduces the action; کوبند is a third-person plural form (classical/poetic) from کوفتن/کوبیدن meaning 'they strike/beat' and functions here as a present/future or hortative in reported speech ('they will/shall strike'). پیلان 'elephants' is the object, جنگی is an adjective 'warlike/war-' qualifying them. بپی is an onomatopoeic or emphatic particle (poetic echo) attached to the verb to evoke the sound of beating. Together: 'they will strike/beat the war-elephants (with a resounding sound).'
segments.s731-g13: همه مردم is the direct object 'all the people.' از شهر بیرون is the prepositional phrase 'out of the city.' کنند is the present/subjunctive/optative third-person plural used in narrative to express future or intended action ('they will/might/shall cause/remove'); combined, the clause means 'they will drive all the people out of the city.'
segments.s731-g14: همه ری 'all of Rey' is the noun phrase object. بپی again is a poetic/onamatopoetic particle echoing the previous phrasing. دشت و هامون are complements meaning 'plain and wasteland'; کنند is the same verb as before ('they will make/turn'). The clause says they will lay waste to all of Rey, turning it into plains and desert.
segments.s731-g15: گرانمایه دستور is a nominal phrase 'the esteemed/precious دستور.' In classical Persian دستور can be an office/title ('commander' or 'minister') as well as 'order'; here it denotes a high-ranking officer. با شهریار 'with the king' indicates accompaniment or that he spoke in the king's presence. This segment sets up the speaker and circumstance.
segments.s731-g16: چنین گفت introduces quoted speech 'thus he said.' کای is a vocative 'O Kay' (an address-title used for Kayanid kings). از کیان یادگار is an appositive phrase 'remnant/ heir/leftover of the Kiān (the Kayanian dynasty)' addressing the king's lineage; یادگار here means 'relic/remnant/legacy.' Together the clause reads as the opening of direct address: 'Thus he said, “O Kay, remnant of the Kayanids...”'
segments.s731-g17: نگه کن is an imperative phrase: نگه (look/heed) + کن (do! — the light imperative auxiliary). که introduces the following clause. شهری بزرگست ری is a poetic word order: شهری (the city) + بزرگست (is great; archaic copula -ست) + ری (Rey, the proper name). The copula is the older -ست rather than modern است.
segments.s731-g18: نشاید is an impersonal/archaic negative: 'it is not fitting' (lit. 'should not be'). که introduces the subordinate clause. کوبند is 3rd person plural present (they strike/beat) from کوبیدن. پیلان (elephants) is the object/agent of the stamping image. بپی is an archaic/contracted adverbial/onomatopoetic element meaning roughly 'by (their) feet / by stomping' (poetic fusion of prepositional idea).
segments.s731-g19: که introduces a reason/explanation clause. یزدان (God) is the subject. دران (در + ان) means 'in that / with that' and links to کار (that work/deed); همداستان is an adjective 'like‑minded / in agreement'. The clause sets up a predication that will be negated in the next segment.
segments.s731-g20: نباشد is the negative copula/verb here ('is not / does not exist [in that state]'), applying to the preceding predicate (همداستان). نه هم is a reinforcing negative connector 'nor/also not'. بر زمین is the locative 'upon the earth' and راستان ('the upright') is the noun phrase: the whole means 'nor are the upright on earth (in agreement)'.
segments.s731-g21: This is an inverted poetic order: به دستور (to the Dastur / to the minister) + گفت (said) + آن زمان (at that time) + شهریار (the king). In prose it would be 'شهریار به دستور آن زمان گفت' — the verb 'گفت' is past tense 'said' and the subject شهریار follows it for poetic effect.
segments.s731-g22: که introduces the content of what was said. بد گوهری is a compound meaning 'of bad disposition / bad nature' (بد modifying گوهری used figuratively as 'character/essence'). باید is the impersonal/modal 'it is necessary / required'. و نابکار adds an appositive/adjective 'and wicked', completing the king's description of the needed man.
segments.s731-g23: که introduces another content clause. یک چند is a temporal phrase 'for a while / for some time'. باشد is the subjunctive/polite present 'let there be / there should be'. بری here functions poetically as 'برای' (for/as/to be) before مرزبان (the marzban, frontier governor) — the phrase means 'let there be for the marzban (i.e. as marzban)'.
segments.s731-g24: یکی مرد = 'one man / a certain man' (یکی as an indefinite 'one / someone'). بی دانش = 'without knowledge' (بی + دانش: ignorant). و بد زبان = 'and ill‑tongued' (bad of tongue = harsh/ill-speaking). This is the description of the man to be put in the marzban post.
segments.s731-g25: بدو گفت بهمن is an inverted, literary order meaning "Bahman said to him": بدو (to him) precedes گفت (said) and بهمن is the speaker. که‌گر is an archaic fusion of که + گر meaning "if" and introduces the subordinate clause whose subject is شهریار (the king).
segments.s731-g26: بخواهد is 3rd-person present/future-subjunctive "wants/will"; the clause is elliptical — نشان functions as the (verbal) object meaning "to show/make known" where the light verb (e.g. کردن/دادن) is omitted. چنین نابکار is a noun phrase "such a wicked one" modifying that omitted action.
segments.s731-g27: بجوییم and آوریم are 1st-person-plural subjunctive/hortative forms (بـ + present stem + -یم) meaning "let us seek" and "let us bring/put (it)"; این را is the definite object (را marks it) and بجا آوریم is an idiom "place/settle properly" (بجا modifies آوریم).
segments.s731-g28: نباید is an impersonal prohibition "one must not / it must not be" and is followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که; بی‌رهنما is an adverbial/ adjectival phrase "without a guide," and آوریم repeats the 1st-plural verb "we bring" (subjunctive), so the clause means "we must not bring (it) unguided."
segments.s731-g29: چنین گفت خسرو is a literary order "thus said Khosrow" (چنین = "thus/so"); که introduces reported speech/content. بسیارگوی is an adjective (or adjectival noun) "very talkative" placed after the reporting clause as part of the description.
segments.s731-g30: نژند اختری are piled descriptors: نژند "base/worthless" and اختری "star‑like / star‑born" characterizing the person. بایدم is an archaic 1st‑person form (باید + -م) meaning "I must/ought (to)"; سرخ موی literally "red‑haired" (سرخ modifies موی). The clause is elliptical (the object of بایدم is implied).
segments.s731-g31: This is a compressed descriptive string: تنش (تن + ـش = his body) سرخ (red) و بینی کژ (and nose crooked). وروی is a fused poetic form here representing و + روی ("and face") and links to the following adjective زشت (ugly). Word order places the possessed noun + possessive clitic before adjectives.
segments.s731-g32: همان is an emphatic demonstrative "that very"; دوزخی modifies روی to mean "hellish face/visage." دور از بهشت is a prepositional phrase "far from paradise." The phrase sums up moral/physical condemnation (figurative distance from heaven).
segments.s731-g65: چوآمد is a fused past/temporal clause marker meaning "when/when he came"; it heads the temporal clause. بری precedes the noun as an adjective/epithet (here a poetic quality, "unruly/untamed"). مرد ناتندرست is a noun phrase (مرد = "man") with the adjective ناتندرست "unsound, sickly" following the noun; Persian allows adjective after noun. The whole segment = "when an unruly, sickly man arrived."
segments.s731-g66: دل و دیده is a coordinated direct-object phrase ("heart and eye"); و simply joins the two nouns. از introduces the cause/ablative phrase ("from/because of"); شرم یزدان is a genitival phrase where یزدان ("God") governs شرم ("shame"). بشست is 3rd singular past of شستن with the prefixed b- (poetic/historical form) and completes the clause: "he washed (them) from the shame of God."
segments.s731-g67: بفرمود (honorific/past) = "he ordered/said"; تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose or command ("that/so that"). ناودانهای بام is the object phrase "the roof-gutters" (ناودانهای = plural with ezafe-like attachment suppressed in poetry; بام = "roof") and functions as the patient of the following verb.
segments.s731-g68: بکندند is the 3rd-person-plural past form with b- (they removed/tore out); it completes the تا-clause introduced earlier. و = "and" links to the result clause. او شد بران شادکام is the consequence: او (he) شد (became) + بران ("thereby/on that") + شادکام ("joyful/fortunate").
segments.s731-g69: وزان پس is a temporal/consecutive phrase meaning "from that (time) on / thereafter" (و + از آن پس compressed). همه گربکان is the accusative object "all the kittens"; in the poetic line the object marker را is attached to the verb as رابکشت (see note). The final verbal idea is the past action of killing: "he killed all the kittens."
segments.s731-g70: This clause reads as a small nominal sentence: دل کد خدایان = "the envious heart of the gods" (کد is "envy", governed by خدایان). ازو is a prepositional/pronominal phrase "toward him / of him" marking the object of the gods' envy. شد درشت is a predicate: شد (became) + درشت ("large/great") = "grew great."
segments.s731-g71: به هرسو is a prepositional phrase meaning "to/into every direction / everywhere." همی‌رفت is an imperfective/past habitual form (the prefix همی- marks continuous or habitual aspect) meaning "he was going / he would go." با رهنمای is an instrumental/accompanying phrase "with a guide."
segments.s731-g72: منادیگری is a verbal noun or collective noun meaning "heraldry / the office of a herald" and functions as the subject or accompanying element. پیش او = "before him / in his presence." بر is a locative/directional preposition ("on/upon/toward") and combines with بپای, an archaic/poetic verb form meaning to stand/watch/abide; together بر بپای indicates the heralds were posted/standing before him.
segments.s731-g73: همی‌گفت‌گر is a poetic, compound expression: همی‌گفت (habitual/iterative past: ‘he kept saying’ or ‘he would say’) plus the conditional particle گر (‘if’), so it introduces a quoted conditional remark ('he would say, “if …”'). ناودانی is the physical downspout/gutter noun, and بجای here means 'instead of / in place of' (marks the alternative about to be described).
segments.s731-g74: ببینی is the subjunctive/present form of دیدن used in conditional clauses introduced by گر, giving 'if you see' (or 'should you see'). وگر is the conditional connective 'or if / but if' linking an alternative condition. گربه‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a cat' (the -ای is the indefinite marker). در is the preposition 'in/inside', and سرای here functions as 'house/household' (the provided lemma senses do not include this meaning).
segments.s731-g75: بدان is best read as بِ + دان, a prepositional phrase 'to/against that / to that (place)' (ب = to/into). بوم و بر is a fixed pair meaning 'land and dwelling / country and home'. آتش اندر زنم places the verb زنم ('I strike/kindle') at the end in typical classical word order; زنم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive/optative with volitional force, here meaning 'I will kindle/set (it) on fire' (i.e. 'I will set fire').
segments.s731-g76: ز is the archaic/poetic preposition 'from/on account of' (here marking relation to those people). برشان = بر + شان 'upon/them'. همی is the iterative/continuous particle ('continually / will') giving an ongoing or emphatic sense to the action. سنگ‌بر functions here adverbially as 'with stones / by hurling stones' (the listed sense entries don't match this use). سرزنم is the verb (سر + زنم) meaning 'I strike/pelt' or, from the lexical entry, 'I rebuke/reproach'; in context it means 'I will pelt/strike (them) (with stones)'.
segments.s731-g77: همی‌جست is an imperfective/past habitual 'he kept seeking' or 'he was looking continually'. جایی که is the noun 'a place / somewhere' plus the relative/conjunction که introducing the subordinate clause ('where / that'). بد modifies 'یک درم' as 'a bad/worthless one dirham' (i.e. 'for a single bad dirham'); درم is the coin dirham.
segments.s731-g78: خداوند is 'God' (or the sovereign/lord, here best read as God). او را is the object pronoun 'him' with the object marker را placed before the verb. فگندی is an archaic/dialectal past form (compare فکندی) meaning 'threw/cast' (the supplied sense list is not lexical here). به غم uses به + abstract noun to mean 'into sorrow' (به = into/towards; غم = sorrow), so the whole clause means 'God cast him into sorrow.'
segments.s731-g79: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; خانه 'houses' is the object/topic; از موش 'because of the mice' gives the cause. بگذاشتند is a past plural verb here meaning 'they left/abandoned' (not merely 'placed'), so the clause means 'everyone deserted their houses because of the mice.' Note the classical word order where the object/topic may precede the verb.
segments.s731-g80: دل 'heart' is used idiomatically with برداشتند 'they took/removed' to mean 'they withdrew their hearts' — i.e. they gave up, forsook, or withdrew their affection/attachment. از بوم آباد 'from the prosperous homeland' names what is forsaken: بوم = homeland/abode, آباد = prosperous/inhabited. برداشتند is past plural 'they took/removed' (here 'they turned away / forsook').
segments.s731-g81: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. باران بدی is a poetic noun + adjective cluster meaning 'severe/bad rain' (بدی as an old adjectival/qualifying element). ناودانی نبود is the predicate: ناودانی (gutter/downspout) as the subject/theme followed by the past negative نبود 'there was not'.
segments.s731-g82: به شهر اندرون is a prepositional/locative phrase 'into/within the city' (به + اندرون). پاسبانی نبود is an existential negative: پاسبانی 'guarding/guards' as the thing absent and نبود the past 'there was not'.
segments.s731-g83: ازان 'from that' introduces the source. زشت بد کامه is a piled set of modifiers (زشت 'vile', بد 'bad') qualifying کامه 'desire'. شوم پی is a nominal phrase: شوم 'ill‑omened/accursed' modifies پی used as 'follower/one who follows'. The whole is a noun phrase functioning as subject of the next verb.
segments.s731-g84: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. آمد is the past tense 'came'. ز درگاه خسرو is a prepositional phrase 'from the presence/court of Khosrow' (درگاه = royal court). بری is a post‑nominal qualifier meaning 'separated/from' or 'away from' (describing the manner/condition of coming).
segments.s731-g85: شد is the past tense 'became'. آن شهر آباد is the subject phrase 'that prosperous city'. یکسر is an intensifier 'entirely' and خراب is the predicate adjective 'ruined'; word order is subject + adverbial intensifier + predicate.
segments.s731-g86: به سر بر is a locative expression 'upon the heads/roofs' (به + سر + the postposition بر). همی‌تافتی is an archaic imperfective/continuous form 'was continually shining/striking' (همی‑ marks ongoing aspect). آفتاب is the subject placed after the verb in this poetic order: 'the sun kept shining/striking upon the heads.'
segments.s731-g87: همه شهر یکسر is an emphatic subject phrase 'the whole city, entirely'. پر از is the fixed construction 'full of' and introduces the contents 'داغ و درد' ('burning grief and sorrow/pain'). There is no copula; پر functions predicatively.
segments.s731-g88: کس اندر جهان is a negative indefinite 'no one in the world' (کس with negated verb implies 'no one'). یاد ایشان کردن is the verbal phrase 'to remember them'; نکرد is the past negative 'did not do', so together 'no one in the world remembered them.'
segments.s731-g49: ببردند is 3rd-person plural past (they carried/took away). زین گونه is a frozen prepositional/qualifying phrase 'in this manner' (زین + گونه = 'this way, such a kind'). مردی is the indefinite direct object 'a man'. برش here is an archaic/poetic sequence where بر + ـش functions to mark the relation to the following clause (roughly 'toward him/away from him'); the clitic ـش (= 'his/him') is attached in poetry and refers forward to the person laughed at in the next phrase.
segments.s731-g50: بخندید is 3rd-person singular past 'he laughed' and here takes a target marked by the preposition ز (poetic 'from/at') so it means 'he laughed at / derided'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. کشور and لشکرش name the targets 'his country' and 'his army', the latter carrying the possessive clitic -ش = 'his'.
segments.s731-g51: بدو گفت خسرو is inverted word order common in older Persian: بدو = 'to him', گفت is the verb 'said' (here reported past), and خسرو is the speaker 'Khosrow' (here functioning as subject/agent). ز کردار بد is a prepositional/causal phrase 'because of/from this bad deed' (کز/ز + noun + adjective).
segments.s731-g52: چه is the interrogative 'what'. داری is 2nd-person singular present 'you have' (what do you have/what's in your mind). بیاد‌ای is a fused poetic vocative/locative phrase (بیاد 'in memory/in mind' with the vocative ای), addressing the addressee; بد بی‌خرد is the vocative epithet 'O bad, foolish one' (بد = 'bad', بی‌خرد = 'unwise/foolish').
segments.s731-g53: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' introducing reported speech. گفت is 'said'. با شاه here means 'to the king' (با used in older Persian with verbs of speech). کز is the relative/causal 'that/from which' linking to the following reason clause. کار بد = 'a bad deed/evil act'.
segments.s731-g54: نیاسایم is 1st-person present/past-negated form meaning 'I do not rest / I have no rest' (an older verbal form expressing lack of repose). و is 'and'. نیست 'is not' (impersonal/existential). با من 'with me' (possession/positional), خرد 'wisdom' — so the clause says 'and wisdom is not with me' (i.e. 'I lack wisdom').
segments.s731-g55: سخن 'speech/words' is the topic; هرچ گویی is a conditional/relative 'whatever you say' with گویی functioning as 'you say' (second-person). دگرگون کنم uses دگرگون (changed/transformed) with کننـم (1st-person present/subjunctive used here as a future/intent) — 'I will change/alter (them)'.
segments.s731-g56: تن و جان مردم = 'the bodies and lives/souls of the people' (مردم is genitive/possessor). پر از خون کنم is a causative/result phrase: پر از = 'full of', خون = 'blood', and کنـم again 'I will make/cause' — together 'I will fill their bodies and lives with blood' (threatening violent result).
segments.s731-g33: یکی here is an indefinite determiner 'a certain'; it precedes the noun مرد. Adjectival modifiers (بدنام, رخساره زرد) follow the noun as is normal in Persian; رخساره زرد literally 'face/complextion yellow' = 'yellowish/pale-faced'. The coordinating و links the two nominal descriptions.
segments.s731-g34: بد اندیش is a compound adjective meaning 'evil‑minded' (بد modifies اندیش). The following coordination و links another adjectival phrase کوتاه دل, where کوتاه 'short' modifies دل 'heart' = 'short‑hearted' (quick‑tempered). پر ز درد uses پر 'full (of)' + ز 'of/from' to mark the content (درد 'pain/sorrow').
segments.s731-g35: همان 'that/same' picks him out as the same person just described. The three qualities are listed in apposition (بد دل, سفله, بی‌فروغ) joined by و: 'evil‑hearted, vile, and without radiance.' Adjectives again follow the (understood) noun.
segments.s731-g36: سرش is سر + possessive clitic ش = 'his head' and is the locus of the quality. پر ز + noun = 'full of (something)' so پر ز کین = 'full of hatred/vengeance'. The second coordinated phrase follows the same pattern: زبان پر دروغ = 'tongue full of lies' (زبان here = 'speech/utterance').
segments.s731-g37: دو چشمش = 'his two eyes' (دو numerative precedes the noun). The adjectives کژ و سبز follow and are coordinated ('crooked/squinting and green'). The phrase دندان بزرگ is another nominal phrase 'large tooth/teeth' describing his dentition; adjective بزرگ follows the noun as usual.
segments.s731-g38: براه اندرون is an adverbial phrase 'toward/into the inner part/inside'; کژ رود is a verb phrase in which کژ 'crooked' modifies the verb رود 'goes' (here third‑person present/past narrative form of 'رفتن' used poetically = 'bends/goes'). همچو گرگ is a simile 'like a wolf.' Note: the printed sense list treats رود as a noun (river) but here it is the verb 'goes/bends', so choose verb reading.
segments.s731-g39: همه موبدان = 'all the mobeds (Zoroastrian priests)'. مانده is a participial/past form meaning 'remained' or 'stood' (here 'stood/was left') and takes ز + pronoun to mark the cause/target: ز + (او) = 'at him/from him' (written contracted as زو). در شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in astonishment/wonder.'
segments.s731-g40: که تا introduces a subordinate clause of extent/result: که تا ... چنین چون گرفت = 'to the point that/so that even ... thus took'. تا here has the sense 'as far as/even' (extent), and که is linking/cause. یاد خسرو is the noun phrase 'the remembrance/memory of Khosrow' as the subject of گرفت (past 'took/seized' / 'was seized'); چنین چون is a doubled adverbial intensifier 'in such a way', and گرفت is past of گرفتن 'to seize/take' used narratively.
segments.s731-g41: همی‌جست is an archaic imperfective past meaning ‘kept searching / was searching’; it sets a continuous past action. هرکس is the subject ‘everyone / each person’ and follows the verb here (old word order is verb → subject). بگرد جهان is an adverbial phrase ‘around/about the world’ with بگرد (search/look about) governing جهان (the world) as the scope of the search.
segments.s731-g42: This is a list of sources/people introduced by the preposition ز ‘from’. شهر کسان is ‘people from the towns/cities’ (شهر + کسان = ‘towns’ and ‘people’). The second part repeats a ‘from’ idea with از and then two nouns/epithets کهان و مهان describing those people (old/ancient and visitors/guests); the whole string enumerates who was searching or who came.
segments.s731-g43: چنان بد که is an idiomatic classical phrase meaning roughly ‘it so happened that…’ (چنان = so/thus; بد here is an archaic particle in the idiom). روزی functions as a temporal adverb ‘one day’ (older verbal root used adverbially). یکی is ‘a certain / one’, and نزد شاه means ‘into the presence of the king’ or ‘before the king’ (نزد = in the presence of).
segments.s731-g44: بیامد is a simple past ‘came’. کزین گونه مردی is a descriptive phrase: کزین ‘of/from this’, گونه ‘kind/sort’, مردی ‘a man’ — together ‘a man of that sort’. به راه is a locative phrase ‘on the road’ or ‘while travelling’ (به + راه = on the path).
segments.s731-g45: بدیدم is a first‑person past ‘I saw’. The following phrase is an elliptical/declarative question or intention: بیارم normally would be the present/subjunctive 'I bring / shall I bring', but the sense list supplied does not include that verbal reading (see senses). به فرمان کی is an interrogative adjunct ‘by whose command?’ (کی here functions as ‘who’). Together this is a reported speech fragment: ‘I saw him; shall I bring him — by whose order?’
segments.s731-g46: بدان is the verb here meaning roughly ‘let (him) know / tell (him)’ (ب + دان), introducing a purpose/result clause. تا is the subordinating particle ‘so that / in order that’. فرستدش is a present/subjunctive form ‘may/might send him’ (subordinate purpose), and خسرو به ری specifies the agent and destination: ‘Khosrow to Ray’ (i.e., that Khosrow send him to Ray).
segments.s731-g47: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past form ‘he commanded/ordered’. تا introduces the purpose/result clause here (‘so that / in order that’). نزد او آورند is a reported-command clause: نزد = ‘in the presence of’, او = ‘him’ (the king), آورند is the subjunctive/imperative form ‘they bring’ used under reported speech.
segments.s731-g48: This second coordinated clause continues the command. وز here is written as one word but functions like و + ز (‘and that/and from’) in older usage (the listed dictionary senses do not directly match, see senses). آنگونه بازی = ‘that kind of play/contest/feat’ (آنگونه = in that way; بازی = play/contest). بکو آورند is a verbal phrase meaning ‘perform/execute/strike/do it’ (بکو is a short verb form ‘strike/perform’ and آورند ‘they bring/do’ — together indicating they should produce that feat before the king).
segments.s731-g1: برآمد is a past verb 'arose/occurred' placed before the temporal phrase. برین is an older fused prepositional/demo- strative combination (بر + این) meaning 'in that' or 'then'; روزگاری ('time, age') is the head noun with the postpositive adjective دراز ('long') following it, a normal Persian order (noun + adjective).
segments.s731-g2: نبد is an older negative past copula (classical variant of نبود) meaning 'there was not / lacked'. گردیه را names the group/assembly (گردیه) with را as the definite/object marker; به + چیزی نیاز is a fixed expression 'need for anything' where به marks the relation and نیاز is the noun 'need'. The whole clause says that the assembly lacked any need.
segments.s731-g3: چنین ('thus') modifies the following verb. می‌همی‌خورد is a literary/habitual verb-form built with the present progressive prefix می‑ and an older verb-form همی‌خورد; here it has the impersonal/experiential sense 'it was so / it befell' rather than a literal 'eat'. با introduces the company 'with/among', and بخردان is the plural of بخرد ('wise ones'), meaning 'the wise'.
segments.s731-g4: This is a coordinated noun phrase: بزرگان 'great ones' and a second noun phrase رزم آزموده ردان. رزم آزموده is a compound adjective (noun رزم 'battle' + past participle آزموده 'tested/experienced') that modifies ردان; together they mean 'those proven in battle' (i.e. battle‑tested warriors).
segments.s731-g5: بدان here is the classical locative/demonstrative 'in that' (literary fusion of preposition + demonstrative). مجلس ('council, assembly') is the place; اندر is another locative 'in/within' (reinforcing the setting). یکی جام ('one/a certain cup') places the indefinite adjective یکی before جام, and بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause states that in that assembly there was a cup.
segments.s731-g6: نوشته is a noun/participle 'written / inscription' functioning as the thing being described; برو here is an archaic contraction of بر + او 'on it' (literary), introducing the locus of the inscription. نام بهرام بود is a simple nominal clause 'the name was Bahram' with بود as the past copula. Altogether: 'on it was written the name Bahram.'
segments.s731-g7: بفرمود is a courtly/honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces the subordinate clause (classical use 'that/so that') marking what he ordered. جام is the object 'the cup'; بنداختند is a verbal form meaning they set/placed/produced the cup (an older/polite past plural), here describing the action taken on the order.
segments.s731-g8: وزان = 'and from that' (و + زان) links the result. هرکسی 'every person / each one' is the subject; دل 'heart' is the direct object of the following verb phrase. بپرداختند is a classical compound past meaning 'they paid/pledged/gave' — here 'they gave/pledged their hearts' (i.e. each man pledged his heart), so the clause records the emotional/ritual reaction to the cup.
segments.s731-g57: سرمایه من is a noun + possessive (noun before pronoun: 'my capital/asset'). دروغست is the archaic/poetic copular form (دروغ + -ست = 'is a lie'). و بس is a short coordinating phrase meaning 'and that is all' / 'and nothing more'. The whole segment states a possession and its being characterized as only falsehood.
segments.s731-g58: سوی راستی is a prepositional phrase 'toward / to the truth' (سوی + noun). نیستم is the 1st person singular negative copula 'I am not'. دست رس is a compound (modern دسترس) meaning 'within hand‑reach / accessible'; here split in the verse but functions as 'within reach'. Together: 'Toward the truth I am not within reach' = 'I cannot reach the truth.'
segments.s731-g59: بدو is the clitic/phrase 'to him' (به + او) placed before the verb; گفت here functions as the report verb 'said' (poetic form/wording); خسرو is the speaker's name. که introduces the content clause that follows. بد اخترت is 'your ill/unfavorable star' (بد=bad, اخترت = اختر + enclitic -ت 'your'), i.e. 'your evil star.' Word order is inverted poetically: 'to him said Khosrow that ...'.
segments.s731-g60: نوشته is used as 'written' / 'that is written' (a nominal/past participial form). مبادا is the negative optative/formula 'may it not be' / 'let it not be'. جزین = 'except this' or 'nothing but this' (here part of the negation). بر سرت is the locative 'upon your head' (بر + سرت). Together they form 'may it not be written upon your head' (i.e. may this ill fate not befall you).
segments.s731-g61: به دیوان is an indirect object/prepositional phrase 'to/for the Diwan' (the royal office or register). نوشتند is the 3rd person plural past 'they wrote/inscribed'. منشور ری = 'the charter/inscription of Ray' (منشور followed by ری, the place name Rey). The order (to‑phrase before verb) is a common poetic/narrative inversion.
segments.s731-g62: ز here marks cause/reason 'because of / from'. زشتی بزرگی is a noun phrase 'great ugliness' or 'the ugliness of greatness' (زشتی modified/qualified by بزرگی). شد is the past 'became / happened'. آن شوم پی = 'that ill (شوم) consequence/following' (پی here 'following/result'); the clause means 'because of great vileness an ill sequel occurred.' Word order is compressed and literary: cause (ز ...) + verb + resulting nominal phrase.
segments.s731-g63: سپاه پراگنده is a noun phrase 'the scattered army' (noun + adjective). او را is the object/topic marker with the pronoun 'him' (او + را), here marking the person to whom the army is entrusted. سپرد is the past verb 'entrusted/handed over' (3rd person). The clause reads 'he entrusted the scattered army to him.'
segments.s731-g64: برفت is a past narrative verb 'he departed / went away' (poetic past). از درو functions as a prepositional adverbial 'from there / away from him' (درو here serving as a locative/contracted form). نام زشتی is 'the shameful name / the name of ugliness' (نام = 'reputation/name', زشتی = 'ugliness/shameful quality'). ببرد (here) is a 3rd singular form of بردن with the prefix ب‑ used in narrative/optative contexts: 'to take away / carry off'; together the final phrase means he went away in order to remove that shameful reputation (or simply 'and removed the shameful name').

sentences.s731-s5: They uttered a curse against Bahram upon that cup, and likewise against the cup-bearer.
sentences.s731-s6: Thus he said that now on the soil of Rey they will beat the war-elephants.
sentences.s731-s7: They will drive all the people out of the city; they will make all of Rey a plain and a desert.
sentences.s731-s8: The esteemed commander, with the king, thus spoke: 'O Kay, remnant of the Kayanids...'
sentences.s731-s9: Take heed, for the city of Rey is great; it is not fitting that it be beaten by the stamping of elephants.
sentences.s731-s10: For God is not of one mind with that deed, nor are the upright on the earth (in agreement).
sentences.s731-s11: The king said to the minister at that time that a man of bad nature is required — wicked and of ill character.
sentences.s731-s12: That, for a short while, there should be as marzban (frontier-governor) a certain man — one ignorant and ill‑tongued.
sentences.s731-s13: Bahman said to him, 'If the king wishes to show (or make known) such a wicked one...'
sentences.s731-s14: Let us search and bring this to its proper place; we must not bring it without a guide.
sentences.s731-s15: Khosrow said thus: 'A very talkative, base, star‑born one — I must have (one) with red hair.'
sentences.s731-s16: His body red, nose crooked, and face ugly — that hellish visage, far from paradise.
sentences.s731-s33: When a sickly, unruly man came, he washed his heart and eyes from the shame of God.
sentences.s731-s34: He ordered that the roof gutters be removed, and he became thereby joyful.
sentences.s731-s35: From that time on he killed all the kittens; the envy of the gods toward him grew great.
sentences.s731-s36: He went everywhere with a guide; a herald (man of heraldry) stood before him.
sentences.s731-s37: He would say, “If you see a downspout instead, or if a cat is in the house…”
sentences.s731-s38: I will set fire to that land and dwelling, and I will hurl stones at them.
sentences.s731-s39: He kept searching for some place where, over a single worthless dirham, God had cast him into sorrow.
sentences.s731-s40: Everyone abandoned their houses because of the mice; they forsook their prosperous homeland.
sentences.s731-s41: When heavy rain fell there were no gutters; within the city there was no guard.
sentences.s731-s42: Out of that vile, ill‑willed desire came an ill‑omened follower who had come from Khosrow’s court.
sentences.s731-s43: That once‑prosperous city became entirely ruined; the sun kept beating down upon the rooftops.
sentences.s731-s44: The whole city was entirely full of grief and pain; no one in the world remembered them.
sentences.s731-s25: They carried off a man in this fashion; he laughed at him — at his country and his army.
sentences.s731-s26: Khosrow said to him, 'Because of this bad deed what do you have in mind, O wicked fool?'
sentences.s731-s27: Thus he said to the king: 'Because of this bad deed I cannot find rest, and wisdom is not with me.'
sentences.s731-s28: Whatever words you say I will alter, and I will make the bodies and lives of the people full of blood.
sentences.s731-s17: There was a certain notorious man with a yellowish face — evil‑minded, short‑tempered, and with a heart full of pain.
sentences.s731-s18: The same ill‑hearted, base, and lackluster man — his head full of hatred and his tongue full of lies.
sentences.s731-s19: His two eyes were crooked and green, and a large tooth curved inward like a wolf's.
sentences.s731-s20: All the mobeds stood astonished at him, so much so that even the remembrance of Khosrow was taken in this way.
sentences.s731-s21: Everyone was searching all around the world — people from the towns, from the ancients and the visitors.
sentences.s731-s22: It so happened that one day a man of that sort came to the king while on the road.
sentences.s731-s23: “I saw (him); shall I fetch him — by whose command? — tell (this) so that Khosrow may send him to Ray.”
sentences.s731-s24: He commanded that they bring him before the king and that they perform that kind of feat.
sentences.s731-s1: A long period then arose; the assembly had no need of anything.
sentences.s731-s2: Thus it was among the wise — great men and warriors tried in battle.
sentences.s731-s3: In that council there was a cup; on it was written the name Bahram.
sentences.s731-s4: He commanded that the cup be brought forward, and from it each person's heart was pledged.
sentences.s731-s29: My principal asset is a lie and nothing more; toward the truth I am not within reach.
sentences.s731-s30: Khosrow said to him: 'May that evil star not be written upon your head.'
sentences.s731-s31: They inscribed a charter to the Diwan of Ray; because of great vileness that ill consequence occurred.
sentences.s731-s32: He entrusted the scattered army to him and departed, so as to take away (remove) the shameful name.
